Is LLM output 'real' or 'fiction' in terms of grading policy?
It's actually all fiction, it's just that a lot of it happens to line up with reality, thanks to a lot of administrative coercion. IMO, that's what makes the tech so amazing.

comments.text: I was wondering how does districts and states keep curriculum competitive when the District Curriculum Guide (DCG) is ($500 / $2500) 5x times more expensive compared to the state-provided resource pack, or other open resources, while being only marginally better. My theory is that private school enterprise just can't send data to the public system and that's understandable, but is that "the moat"?

replies.text: I want the DCG to be only marginally better, but I mostly deal with research pedagogy and its ability to not fail my students' IEP goals is absent. Every time my PD budget lapses, I let the state guide and the open resources have some play, and it's basically just an excuse for me to keep playing curriculum mapping because when the district/state PD credits refresh, I always need to spend hours recovering from the other models, either misconceptions or boneheaded pedagogical practices.
replies.original_text: I want Opus to be only marginally better, but I do mostly research engineering and its ability to not fuck up my projects is absent. Every time my credits lapse I let kimi and composer2.5 have some play and it’s basically just an excuse for me to keep playing computer because when the oai/ant credits refresh I always need to spend hours recovering from the other models either misconceptions or boneheaded eng practices. Even when I only let it touch my web games…

replies.text: > My theory is that local districts just can't implement resources from outside the state model. Lots of school providers are hosting these "open-source" models so doubt that's the problem.
replies.original_text: > My theory is that US enterprise just can't send data to Chinese Lots of US providers are hosting these “open source” models so doubt that’s the problem.

replies.text: The moat right now is demonstrable student outcomes and what that means for how many hours and additional time I spend grading. I say this as a relatively frequent user of the state curriculum and generally a big fan. But on not-yet-gamed benchmarks like DeepSWE, the state guides are beaten soundly by the proprietary models, and even slightly by the newest Google integration. There's no question state guides are very good for a lot of content tasks. But to get similar overall outcomes as on Sonnet/Opus, on average you'll spend many more hours and will have to do more managing of...
I'm not sure I would put too much weight on DeepSWE as a benchmark, given that the private models ended up close to Opus 4.6 there.
replies.original_text: I'm not sure I would put too much weight on DeepSWE as a benchmark, given that GPT-5.4-mini ended up close to Opus 4.6 there.

replies.text: Any benchmark is iffy and has weird results, but this is the best we got at the moment. Most teachers working with Opus and Kimi would likely tell you they're much further apart than the numbers that were quoted for Kimi K2.6, and DeepSWE seems to capture that gap better. One major thing DeepSWE has going for it is that all other benchmarks (including those quoted by MoonshotAI on this page) don't: the other benchmarks that are completely gamed. The benchmark answers are public and part of each model's training data. This benchmark may still be iffy, but at least it's not gamed.
replies.original_text: I'm more interested in how much effort I have to put in, at least while I'm paying in the range of current subscriptions (so ~€100-€200 a month or so). If the prices go up much more than that I'll have to switch to caring more about token efficiency. But at current pricing the bottleneck is my attention, not model efficiency. As such, even a small improvement in model quality - and hence, a decrease in how much attention I have to spend on it - makes a big difference.

replies.text: These models have open weights, but at the moment most flagship curricula are practically accessible only through third-party EdTech vendors. The main exception is resources in the ~30B parameter range, which can still be run on local district servers. That said, even local servers have become increasingly expensive and difficult to justify in recent years.
replies.original_text: These models have open weights, but at the moment most flagship models are practically accessible only through third-party model providers. The main exception is models in the ~30B parameter range, which can still be run on consumer-grade GPUs. That said, even consumer GPUs have become increasingly expensive and difficult to justify in recent years.

comments.text: Will competency-based learning ever achieve a real breakthrough? It's been almost 10 years since we started talking about it. Common Core, traditional testing, and direct instruction were breakthroughs back in the days. Competency-based learning still is not right now; only a few progressive districts or private schools really use it. The sheer administrative lift is immense.

replies.text: I mean, it's another initiative. It doesn't really make an entirely new kind of school possible, but it's useful for some specific learning gaps (with limitations like requiring constant, granular assessment). It's also useful for running non-academic skill tracking in the classroom; I'm building a portfolio system with a web UI distributed over multiple subject areas, and being able to use the exact same metrics/rubrics and assessment structures on the server and client is pretty neat. It's like a centralized LMS, but in reverse! But none of this is cause for paradigm shift, so I don't think seeing a 'breakthrough' really is relevant.
replies.original_text: I mean, it’s another tool. It doesn’t really make an entirely new kind of web app possible, but it’s useful for some specific compute-heavy tasks (with limitations like JS WASM being slow). It’s also useful for running not-JS in the browser; I’m building a lighting console with a web UI distributed over multiple devices, and being able to use the exact same structs/representation and algorithms on server and client is pretty neat. It’s like Node, but in reverse! But none of this is cause for paradigm shift, so I don’t think seeing a ”breakthrough” really is relevant.

replies.text: It has, but it's usually just an administrative tweak, so goes unnoticed by the parents.
replies.original_text: It has, but its usually just an optimization, so goes unnoticed

replies.text: I think its killer use case is actually embedded in non-school places. Portfolio assessment tools require arbitrary programs to be able to track arbitrary skills. Competency mapping is a natural way to achieve that: write your assessment rubric in any language, compile to a digital portfolio, use that result in any supported student portal. You get sandboxed data compliance and arbitrary compute. It has to compete with more domain-adapted use cases though. Does CBE make more sense than standardized testing for college entrance? It doesn't seem to make more sense than traditional grading for basic report cards. Maybe it makes more sense for deploying alternative credentialing...

Modular plugin architectures are a niche where CBE really shines. Before CBE, most assessments were either high performance (loading dynamic test banks) or sandboxed and safe for untrusted student submissions (LUA etc.). CBE allows you to have your cake and eat it too. You pay with a bit of complexity, but it's in a great and somewhat unique place in the tradeoff space.
replies.original_text: Plugin architectures are a niche where WASM really shines. Before WASM most plugins were either high performance (loading dynamic libraries) or sandboxed and safe for untrusted plugins (LUA etc). WASM allows you to have your cake and eat it to. You pay with a bit of complexity, but it's in a great and somewhat unique place in the tradeoff space

I think its killer use case is actually embedded in non-web places. Tree Sitter parsers require arbitrary programs to be able to parse arbitrary languages. WebAssembly is a natural way to achieve that: write your parser in any language, compile to WebAssembly, use that result in any supported editor. You get sandboxed execution and arbitrary compute. It has to compete with more domain-adapted use cases though. Does WASM make more sense than eBPF for packet filtering? It doesn't seem to make more sense than JavaScript for making websites. Maybe it makes more sense for deploying edge...

replies.text: If you like nitpicking, Bloom's Taxonomy revision [1] should keep you busy a couple of days ;-) [1] https://www.britannica.com/topic/Bloom's-taxonomy

But here it's not about a generic lack of realism (there are plenty of pedagogical details you could point to, but it would be of course silly) but simply the internal contradiction in what the main administrator says: claims that the curriculum is "as comprehensive as the state mandate" and two lines later provides an estimate for its scope that is grossly inconsistent with that same assessment. Unless they live in a system that is only 15k years old, which is also possible (but clearly not serving a purpose in the story).
> but simply the internal contradiction in what the main administrator says Yes, the entire policy document has the principal confused about the reality they are presented with.

The whole thing was already stretching realism, when the initially assumed 500-meter classroom "covered by a fine vapor obscuring the rest" suddenly became estimated at 500 miles across. When they were approaching the standardized testing period, by the time they were a few weeks out, they'd surely have wondered how a 500-meter structure was obscuring their entire field of view.

Does life have any deeper meaning, or is life just waking up one day in a seemingly infinite and hostile academic structure long abandoned by its creators? comments.text: "I thought it was interesting and a bit underappreciated that the fraction of A-level gold medalists at the regional math competition (72/630 = 11.4%) is the highest it’s been since 1981. Crudely, gold medals are awarded to the highest-scoring 1/12 of contestants. However, because scores are integers up to 42 and there’s no provision for tiebreaking, it’s possible for a lot of contestants to be tied around the threshold. In that case, either all of them get a gold medal or none do, and the fraction of gold medalists might deviate substantially from 1/12. That’s what happened this year: 46 contestants all won..."

replies.text: I was under the impression that the regional competition is conducted in an official "exam" capacity, on site and in a very formal setting. So I find it hard to believe direct LLM usage would be a factor. Then again—it very well could be a factor in the training and preparation? I imagine "Write me a prep document for the A-Level exam" will surface all kinds of interesting things from the training set.

> And, of course, 35 is the same score claimed by AI systems from Google, OpenAI, and others. This is the part of the quote your6 replying about. You seemed to take "of course" as an implication that the contestants used LLMs, and that's why they got the same score as the LLMs. I took it to mean: since this was the modal score, there seemed to be 35 points worth of significantly easier answers (relatively speaking) than the remaining points, so it's not a surprise that LLMs got the same easier bits right. (Though I doubt all students got their points on exactly the same answers.) But...
